An interactive paper

Moving the Wall

Two streams that each look like noise, secretly moving as one. Place stones — black and white alternate — and watch E1, a five-number equation, answer locally: the red boxes are where it expects the reply. That local response is the coupling this paper chases past the linear wall.

Black to play
red boxes = E1's top picks  ·  actual move  · 
scroll to see how it works↓
01 · The suspicion

Each color looks like noise. Together they are one object.

Strip away the rules and follow one color's coordinates over time. A simple predictor can barely hold onto the sequence; to that lens it looks like noise. The same is true of the other color alone.

Yet they are obviously not independent: white answers black, black presses white. Across thousands of pro games, black's last stone explains roughly 44% of the predictable part of white's next move. That single measurement is the whole thesis.

a pro game replaying — stones above, one color's x-coordinate as a "noise-like" trace below
interlude · the corpus

Every game at once

Each point is one stone: board position on the floor, move number rising as the vertical axis. Hundreds of pro games stacked together. Structure hides in the haze — that is what the rest of the paper extracts.

—
pro stones · color = move number
Play with it → Interactive explorer 3D move-cloud Compare cohorts E1 model board
move 1
02 · The equation E1

A weak predictor, and a hard ceiling

E1 scores every empty point with five local-geometry features — above all, "how close is this to the opponent's last stone?" — and a softmax turns the scores into a probability map (the heat). The bright spot is its top pick; the yellow ring is what was actually played.

Read as a predictor it captures about 2.4 of the 8.49 bits a move can carry, then plateaus (a deep net reaches ~4.5). Prediction was the wrong job. But that leading weight — answer near the opponent's last stone — is the coupling itself, written as a coefficient.

03 · The meter

A coupling meter with a zero that can't be fooled

Coupling is a difference of two predictors: how much better you predict one stream once you add the other's past, in bits. The danger is spurious correlation — two independent random walks can look strongly correlated by accident.

So the meter never trusts the raw number. It compares against a surrogate that destroys the alignment but keeps each stream's own statistics. Below: two independent walks. Their apparent correlation swings high, yet the calibrated meter reads ≈ 0. It does not get fooled.

apparent |corr| — · meter —
partial correlation ρ²drag — both rise together
04 · The wall

Generalize the meter, and it becomes Granger

Asked what it is mathematically, the meter collapses onto Granger causality: the bits-gap is a monotone transform of the Granger F-statistic, so they always agree.

And a 2009 theorem (Barnett-Barrett-Seth) proves that for jointly Gaussian processes Granger equals transfer entropy, TE = ½F. So for linear-Gaussian coupling, Granger is the wall: nothing is recoverable beyond it.

gap = −½·log₂(1−ρ²)  ·  F ∝ ρ²/(1−ρ²)  ·  TE = ½·F
05 · What the wall depends on

The wall is Gaussian, not absolute

The equivalence needs Gaussianity. The wall is really linear-in-the-mean: coupling through the mean is caught completely, however non-Gaussian the noise; coupling through the variance (volatility spillover) is invisible to Granger.

And away from Gaussian, the gap is only a lower bound: ½F ≤ TE. The blue is what Granger sees; the red margin is coupling that can live past the wall in higher moments or nonlinear shape. Drag to open it.

Gaussian / linear-in-meannon-Gaussian / nonlinear
06 · How far past Granger baduk lives

I don't break the wall. I move it.

Baduk is not Gaussian — its moves are bounded integers — so it can live in that margin. The scalar lens (one axis at a time) sees 0.44 bits of linear coupling. A board-aware lens recovers +0.06 more, and the gain is purely nonlinear: local response.

White's reply lands within one point of black 22% of the time, within two 41%, within three 56%; median white–black distance 2.24 vs 9.06 under a shuffled null. Sharp, not smooth (a forest recovers it; polynomials catch only 4%) and shaped by the board edge.

This corrects an earlier reading where baduk looked flat against the wall. That was an artifact of the scalar lens, too coarse to see 2-D shape. The leftover never vanishes; it shrinks toward the transfer-entropy ceiling.

07 · Where systems sit

One decomposition, many systems

The coupling splits into three terms: L1 same-axis linear, L2 cross-axis linear, L3 nonlinear geometric. The wall is L1 + L2 — proper multivariate Granger. Only L3 lies past it. Baduk is L1 plus a little L3, with essentially no L2.

A different system sits elsewhere. In team sports a defender mirroring an attacker is an L2 relationship; spacing and formation are L3. The falsifiable prediction: soccer should show more L2, basketball more L3 — the same meter, the same calibrated zero, separates them. (Untested; no tracking data yet.)

08 · What this is, honestly

It began as noise that wasn't, and ends at a wall I keep pushing back.

I did not build a new detector. I re-derived the Granger / transfer-entropy family from an unusual door, a noise-like board game, and measured how far baduk's coupling lives past the linear wall. A plain Granger test matches the meter at lower cost; the value is the route and the controlled measurement — and a decomposition that should travel to any system where agents answer one another's positions, from wild baboons to, in principle, team sports.

바둑 / Baduk · 圍棋 Wéiqí · 囲碁 Igo (Go)
